title: Flawed Solar Cells That Work Better
summary: Scientists have discovered that some solar cells made from a material called lead-halide perovskites work really well even though they have many flaws. These solar cells are cheap to make and can turn sunlight into electricity almost as good as the expensive silicon solar cells we use today. Researchers at ISTA found that the flaws in perovskites actually help electricity move through them better. This is surprising because silicon solar cells need to be very pure to work. The study shows that internal forces in perovskites push electrical charges apart, so they don't get stuck. This could help make better and cheaper solar panels in the future. Perovskites were discovered a long time ago but only recently became popular for solar energy. They are also used in lights and X-ray machines. The research helps solve a mystery about why these flawed materials are so efficient.

a type of material used to make solar cells
energy that powers things like lights and gadgets
hidden pushes or pulls inside a material

questions.question: What do solar cells made from perovskites have that helps them work well?
How do perovskites compare to silicon solar cells in cost?
What did researchers find inside perovskites that pushes charges apart?
What is the main job of a solar cell?
Where did the researchers work who studied perovskites?
Why are perovskites surprising to scientists?

background_read: Solar cells are devices that capture sunlight and convert it into electricity to power homes and devices. Silicon solar cells are common but expensive because they need to be very pure. Perovskites are a new type of material that can be made cheaply and have flaws, yet they perform almost as well as silicon. Researchers are studying how these flaws help electricity move, which could lead to cheaper and more efficient solar energy in the future.
Article_Structure: WHO: Researchers at ISTA studied perovskites.
WHAT: They found that flaws in perovskites help solar cells work better.
WHERE: The study was done at the Institute of Science and Technology Austria.
WHY: To understand why cheap, flawed materials can be efficient for solar energy.

perspectives.description: Researchers solved a mystery about why perovskites work well despite flaws.
